sentences.text: My name is Irving, I-R-V-I-N-G, Eisner, E-I-S-N-E-R.
My birth date is March the 10th, 1926.
I was born in a small village called [?
Sojmo, ?] [?
S-O-J-M-O, ?] in Czechoslovakia, in the Carpathian Hills.
Before we go on to talk about your pre-war life in Sojmy, I wanted to ask you, was the name Irving Eisner the name that you were born with?
I was born with a Hebrew name, Yitzhak.
And as our part of this particular part of the world was changed, the names was also changed.
I recall I was once called Ignatz, I-G-N-A-T-Z, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and Yitzhak, and today, Irving.
I don't remember more names.
We'd like to learn from you about your family.
Tell me about your father.
What was his name?
My father's name was Joseph, J-O-S-E-P-H, Joseph Eisner.
He was a child, one boy of-- and five sisters.
He lost his parents, he told me, very young.
In fact, I only remember a little bit of one of my grandparents, his father, called Nahum.
He was nine when he lost his father.
He was 11 when he lost his mother.
And because to keep up some supporting, his sisters, he went to work in stores all over, what he could.
And he succeeded.
And all his life he was only in business.
From the very beginning till the very end, his major business was lumber.
We had our own forests and he became very famous.
And considering that time, he was also very rich in the lumber.
And he was succeeding to marry out his five sisters.
And to each sister, he build a house out of wood.
He was so successful.
He was an Orthodox man, modern.
Didn't have a beard.
He went to pray, as I recall, morning and evening, to a synagogue.
We had one little synagogue.
He built it, matter of fact.
He gave the wood.
He had his own Sefer Torah.
His own.
To his wedding he made himself write a Sefer Torah.
And one Sefer Torah was from the community.
And he kept up the community.
He was modern Orthodox.
Strictly in kashrut, and Shabbat.
And we were brought up the same way.
As far as I-- I know, he was also born in Sojmy.
So was his wife, my mother, [INAUDIBLE], or Feiga.
She was also born in the same village.
Yes.
As far as I know, they never moved until the tragedy of the Holocaust.
We moved for a few years to a larger city.
We only bought a house there, to my bar mitzvah.
But we never left the village.
We still had the village.
Our roots were in the village.
And we can say we lived there all the time.
Yeah.
My mother was a lovely woman.
Her name was Feiga Tzippora.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was great.
Her maiden name was Katz.
And she arrived at the age of 79, to Auschwitz to Birkenau, with a little baby on her arm with her friend.
They killed her.
She was a great woman.
I-- I only know she cared.
She wouldn't enjoy too much luxury.
Rather, to help for the poor.
One little case, what I always remember, that when the Hungarians occupied our place, my father became even more wealthier, richer.
Because he made business with Hungarians.
They opened up the rivers to Hungary.
And we delivered woods by the water.
He became very, very rich.
And so he made, for my wife, a Persian coat.
At that time, to have a Persian coat, it was something like, to have today an airplane or something.
And I remember when he asked her several times to go-- to come with him to the city for entertainment, to have a good time.
She never, never, never, put on that coat.
Her words were, how can I go for entertainment and have a good time, when the people of the village haven't got what to eat?
She never put it on.
Twice a month-- I was the oldest of my family.
There was only one grocery store-- and she took me.
There was no lights in the village.
And we went, a Thursday night, to the grocery.
And she told-- his name was Moshe, Moshe Neuman.
He had a little horse with a wagon.
And we said, Moshe, tonight or tomorrow morning, 10 kilograms of potatoes to Avraham, to [?
Avromseh, ?] he was called. 50 kilograms of potatoes to [?
Jakob. ?] Five kilograms of sugar to this family.
And we delivered.
He delivered.
And he wrote down what we owe him, the money.
My mother didn't pay cash.
And I remember when the month come to an end-- came, she had to pay.
She ask my father, Joseph, I need money.
So he turned his head like this.
You need again, money?
But he gave her.
He gave her.
That was my mother.
She worried if people don't have to eat, she wouldn't be able to enjoy life.
And when I went to school, when I left home, even so I was in boarding room.
She always made me a package.
The delicious cookies who can stay for a few days.
She was, if there is such a thing, and there is, I believe, an [NON-ENGLISH], a woman of valor.
That was my mother.
And there is a [NON-ENGLISH], they must be [? her. ?] Because what she did for people, only God knows the truth.
That was my mother.
Yes.
It was-- it as-- it was carried on.
It was carried on.
And today, I'm already a zayde myself.
Today I'm still singing, with my family, the same songs Friday night and Saturday at lunch, what my father and my mother was singing with us many, many, many years ago.
Her kindness and her behavior, her conduct, somehow reflected on me for sure.
On me for sure.
I can never raise my voice to another person.
I'm ready to help.
But to damage or to hurt somebody, I just can't.
Even if he's not a Jew.
I just can't.
And I think it was from the home, from my mother, as well as my father.
But, you know, a mother is a mother is a home.
A mother is called [NON-ENGLISH].
A mother is called a house.
Why?
Because she is responsible for the upbring of the children.
That was our mother.
And-- and much to my spirit and strength that I survived, I brought it with me from my home, from my mother's home, and my father's home, and from my zayde's home.
That gave me lots of strength.
And I'm trying my best to continue as much as-- at least as long as I live, that my grandchildren should continue, and some do, the same way as we were taught at home.
It might be old fashioned.
But to me, it's better than the new fashion.
I believe in it.
Yeah.
It's kind, also.
Well, how-- we were four children.
I-- I was the oldest, 1926, a boy. '28, sister, my sister who is a survivor.
She is in Israel.
After that was Nahum, a little brother, 1930.
A little sister, '32.
For 10 years, there was no children.
In 1943, my mother gave birth to our twin sisters.
And she would not nurse both of them.
Her breasts-- she couldn't breastfeed only one, because she had an accident.
Once one of the children were crying.
And, I said before, we didn't have lights at home.
We had only, kind of, lamps.
And she took-- she were thinking she taking the baby to breastfeed, and she took the lamp.
And she burned her breast.
So we had to give away one child to a friend.
So when we arrived to Birkenau, my mom arrived only with one child on her arm.
And at the selections she yelled out very loud, to warn the Germans.
Supposedly it was Mengele, the angel of death.
And she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I'm 39 years old.
I want to work.
Please, let me live.
[NON-ENGLISH].
That was the last voice of my father.
And she went away with a baby on her hand.
So was my little sister and my little brother.
I never saw them since.
Yeah.
More than religious, yes.
The same way as my mother, was very supportive in helping the poor.
So was my father in a different way.
For example, in the village we cared, one from another.
First of all, most of-- first of all, the Jews and the Christians in that village lived very, very, very friendly, as one family.
I mentioned to someone that my mother gave birth to six children.
And the midwife was a Christian lady.
No doctor, nothing, because she helped.
I remember her.
She used to pat my-- my mother Feiga, [NON-ENGLISH].
I can mention her words.
Which means, Feiga, don't be afraid.
Don't be afraid.
God is with you.
Don't be afraid.
And the baby was born.
Six of them.
So she did this to everyone in the village.
We lived very, very friendly.
Why I'm saying this, because many of the villagers worked for my father, Jews and Christians.
So we were very, very intermingled.
We were very close to another.
And we cared for one another.
And you know, we had a doctor who was in charge of 20 or 30 villages.
One doctor.
I remember a case like today, when one Jew, his name was Shmuel Steinberg, [HEBREW], he has a hernia.
And he had a terrible attack.
And you didn't have medicines or doctors or-- the first hospital was about 80, 90 kilometers away from our village.
I can see now, when I think about it, my father taking Shmuel on his shoulder.
Taking him to the bus station, going 60 kilometers with a bus, 20 kilometers with a train to the first hospital, staying with him, left him to be operated, and he brought him home.
So that's how-- that's how we-- we-- we lived.
And my father, because he had, also, the money, people during the war, ran from Poland through the mountains.
They had the address, where to come.
It was our place, to sleep over a night.
And my father, somehow, took them to the first train station and sent them to Budapest, to the underground.
Maybe, they will be saved And so tens and tens of people were helped, even during the war.
And before the war, he was very supportive for schools, [NON-ENGLISH], for rabbis, wherever he could.
He was a very, very generous and [NON-ENGLISH] giver man.
All they used to say, I remember, even in Israel, he used to say, Feiga, the more I give, I can feel the more I'm getting back.
Always that was his word.
Because sometimes, you know, he-- [? when it is that ?] [? they ?] used to giving money for building synagogues.
So he-- we-- and my second mother used to ask him, why do you do this?
He said, I know, I feel, the more I give, the more I'm getting back.
So they were great people, my father and my mother.
I wish I shall continue going their-- their way.
Tell me about the schools that you attended in Sojmy.
Yeah.
Sojmy was only one school, which was a Catholic school.
And we went to that school, Jews and non-Jews.
And we were again, we were-- there was no discrimination or anything whatsoever.
We lived together.
We studied together.
Fine.
The school was from eight o'clock in the morning till one o'clock, about 1:00.
Then I went to cheder, the Jewish cheder.
When I turned 10 or 11, I went, in the morning, also from 6:00 till 8:00.
So 7:30, went to public school and then back to cheder.
And that was-- this was going on till I became bar mitzvah.
I became bar mitzvah, I left the village.
I went to a city called Khust.
60 kilometers exactly.
And I went to a Hebrew talking day school, [HEBREW].
Very similar to what we have today in Toronto, associated or other school, they speak only Hebrew.
And there I learned till 1942.
In 1942, I completed my junior high school.
I went to-- to the high school, to [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] or [NON-ENGLISH], to the gymnasium.
Gymnasium was called here, I think, it's called in English, a high school, Hebrew high school, also again, [HEBREW].
And there I went till 1943.
And-- actually 1944, April, 1944.
And then I was taken back to-- to home, to the ghetto, and from the ghetto to Birkenau.
From here, from Toronto, I was sent three times to Bar-Ilan.
Three summers, I was studying in Bar-Ilan.
And since I'm in Canada, 1953, I'm teaching.
And I'm still doing this.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
I cannot-- I was young.
Maybe it was always that I-- I did not.
I never saw anything different until 1940, '41, when the Hungarians marched into our place.
Even though with us, as I mentioned before, they made good business.
But already, you could feel the beginning of anti-Semitism and hate against the Jews.
And the same people who were so friendly to us, they didn't show yet.
But it began, already, to be build in them, the hate against the people in the same village.
Very--
Yes.
Very small.
I-- When I came home from the camp, I wrote a little booklet in Yiddish, the language that we spoke at home, plain Yiddish.
And I named it [YIDDISH], 'My Little Village, Sojmy.' And then I went from house to house.
I remember our house was the first coming from the city.
And I went family to family.
And we were 16 families, all together.
The non-Jews were about double, about 32.
We were one third.
And I wrote down each family, how-- their incomes, their living, from what they made a living.
And I also figured out that we were 115 living souls, children and so on and so on and so on.
Unfortunately, only 15 came back.
And for those 15, I sent them out those booklets which I wrote.
That was a whole little community.
But it was one family.
They used to say there was one man.
He died at the age of, I remember, close to 100.
It was the first time I went to a cemetery.
They called him the Feter Meyer.
His name was Meyer.
But everybody called him the Feter, Feter means uncle.
Because he said that the whole village is built around-- around him, or relatives.
A synagogue, a little house, and every family had their own little field.
Some had bigger ones, [NON-ENGLISH], inheritance from their ancestors.
Some had smaller, but everybody had a little garden with vegetables.
Because-- I told my kids in school-- you could have $1,000 in your pocket.
And you couldn't go buy a liter of milk.
Because it's not just a thing to go to buy milk.
There was no store for milk.
Or to guide-- or go to buy two kilos of meat.
There was not such a thing, a butcher shop.
So everybody had a little farm, chickens, eggs, vegetables.
And they made a living mostly from farming and lumber.
Like I said before, to help work in the forest or deliver the woods and so on, from lumber and from agriculture.
That was the little businesses in our village.
But no one was a beggar.
Because in the village used to be a lot of [NON-ENGLISH], beggars from all the villages.
Our village was poor.
But not one went out of the house to beg, as many others did.
Tell me about Shabbat in your home.
If I was a guest in your home, what-- what smells what I smell?
What food would I eat?
You would feel very good.
I promise you.
If you will come now to my Shabbat, you will feel good too.
Because the [NON-ENGLISH] that we make in our Sojmy, my wife does it today.
But I said to my friends, I don't remember one Shabbos without having-- guests was fine.
A guest like you would-- perfect.
But we had beggars, [NON-ENGLISH].
And they used to wait at the shul.
They knew Joseph Eisner, my father, is a rich man.
Nobody calls them for Shabbat, because there was hardly for them, what to eat.
So they knew they're not going to sleep in the synagogue.
Eisner, Joseph, was going to call them.
So we came home.
And speaking first about them-- and my mother, [HEBREW], used to say, Joseph, [NON-ENGLISH].
Bring one, bring two, three of them.
Why?
Not because it wasn't what to eat.
Because we gave them all-- I had a bedroom with my sister.
And there was a very beautiful cabinet.
The top were Talmud-- the Talmud, the books, leather band, leather covered.
And the other side was desserts, plums, peaches.
You know, in case we need it.
And those people ate and drank as much as I did.
They went in that night.
As they broke the-- the glass of the door and they dirtied our house.
And we had a live in.
We could afford, at that time, to have a live in.
Since I was born, her name was Anna, a Christian girl.
She lived with us till the Germans took us away.
She was married.
She didn't want to move out.
And she had to clean, and clean.
And my mother had to clean.
They brought in dirt and lice and so on.
There was no Shabbos without beggars.
But if we had a guest, first of all, you would feel good, because as soon you walked into the house, you had a good-- first of all, the table was beautiful.
Covered, my mother was in a white [NON-ENGLISH], you know, covered.
The children were dressed nice.
And the songs, you know, Shalom Aleichem and the Kiddush.
It was something-- it's so-- it was soaked into you-- into-- into your-- into your body, into your system.
You would have to sing with us.
You would have to eat with us and have a good time.
And same as a Shabbat, Special food, [NON-ENGLISH] and fish.
And we were blessed.
Our village had mineral waters.
Like today, you have seltzer.
What we had, wells coming out with these waters.
And every family had a balloon, like a bottle, it was called, with that water.
And no matter how much [NON-ENGLISH] you ate or kugel, you drank a glass of that water, you would go to sleep and nothing will happen.
It was beautiful.
Shabbos, we went for a sleep later on.
We kids went to play.
Everybody went to shul.
Not because they were so religious, but there was a custom.
Everybody must go to shul.
And my father made a custom that every boy, after bar mitzvah, he has to sing one chapter as a [NON-ENGLISH], and writing, you know, like, and going around in circles.
Everybody had to be there.
Not because they were so religious, because that was the life as a village.
And then [NON-ENGLISH].
And then even for Saturday night we made a [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother lit candles.
We start blessing, naturally.
It's a-- it was a [NON-ENGLISH], a yontiff.
So the Shabbos, really, as they say, the Zohar says, that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Which means, the sixth day of the weeks are taking in the spirit from one day.
That is Shabbos, spiritual.
That's why we say today is the first day of Shabbos.
Today [HEBREW] Shabbat.
Every day you mention what?
Shabbat.
Because that gives you spirit.
Oh, it's coming.
It's coming.
It's coming, The next Shabbos is coming.
So really, if you participate in such a Shabbos, you will wait to get ready for the next Shabbos.
Especially, the village of the very poor.
They didn't see a piece of meat the whole week.
They didn't see a piece of white bread the whole week.
And Shabbos came.
They had a soup, a chicken soup.
And little piece of chicken meat.
Oy, it was a [NON-ENGLISH].
I remember a man, Yosel the [NON-ENGLISH], Yosel the shoemaker.
He used to go-- I remember, once I was with him because-- because my parents used to-- every spoon, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
Every spoon with soup, used to say, it's [SPEAKING HEBREW].
It was the honor of Shabbos.
That was the Shabbos in our small village.
No.
It was not.
No.
It was Orthodox.
The villagers, what happened there, you know, they left according [NON-ENGLISH], because the [NON-ENGLISH].
Because the [NON-ENGLISH] lived in those hills, the Carpathian Hills, not far from us.
And the villagers took the idea of [NON-ENGLISH].
Means you don't have to know too much Talmud and Torah.
The meaning is the heart.
The heart.
If your heart is close to God, you're closer to God than the one who knows-- who knows what, and he's not close to God.
So [NON-ENGLISH], that was the system of our village.
Serve God, enjoy.
And our villagers used to dance and sing.
And even if they didn't have what to eat.
So when they went to the synagogue, it's not because they want big-- big Talmudic scholars.
They didn't know different.
They had to dress [NON-ENGLISH].
Because that is the way they were brought up.
They couldn't translate, maybe, a page in Talmud.
But they, with all their heart, they believed in [NON-ENGLISH], in faith in God.
And that was enough for them.
And that's why they were a happy group.
They were a happy group.
That's how we lived.
In some ways, yes.
For the adults.
Not the children so far as the adults.
No-- the-- the people were very poor.
Like I said, some of the Christian children, most of them, they had one shirt only, till they're about 8, 10 years old, nothing more.
And the same with some Jewish kids.
They very, very poor.
So the Jewish kids were a little bit better dressed for Shabbat.
Again, not many could afford it.
I remember if my father would not buy new shoes for his five sisters' kids for Pesach, they wouldn't have new shoes, even though their husbands worked for them.
It was very hard.
Did you have a mikveh--
Oh, yes.
We had a mikveh.
And we had to heed the mikveh on our own.
That means every Friday a different family had to heed the mikveh.
It was a big [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] means like a big pot in the water.
And it was heated with wood.
And this form tubs of water.
And when you came out of that mikveh, you could almost choke from the smoke and the steam.
But we went.
Everybody went to the mikveh.
[LAUGHS] Sure, we had a mikveh.
It was Yiddish.
Yiddish.
Only Yiddish.
In the street and the school we spoke Ukrainian, Ukraine.
Only from the high school we learned Czech.
But first, Ukraine.
Depends with whom.
If-- with-- with the people in the city in the village, he speak Ukraine with the non-Jewish.
With the Jewish people, only Yiddish.
And later on, with the Hungarians.
He had to learn-- My father knew, a little bit, Hungarian, because of the First World War.
It was [INAUDIBLE], and he belonged to Hungary.
So he still remembers a little bit of the language of the First World War.
I didn't know a word.
Not a single letter.
I had to learn it.
So he spoke with them in the language they understood.
But among the Jewish people, only Yiddish.
In cheder, we learned Yiddish.
And your father's five sisters, they lived and remained in Sojmy?
No.
They were spread out, different villages.
Two-- two lived in Sojmy.
And the three lived in different villages, not far away from us.
Very, very poor.
Very, very poor.
No.
Not-- not was-- not only our family, but everybody separate.
Everybody separate, celebrated.
Well, somehow for Yontiff and Shabbos, everybody managed to have a little bit better food than during the week.
Oh, my bar mitzvah, I hardly remember it.
I was born in 1926, so when was my bar mitzvah? 1930-- '39?
Right?
Already, it began already to-- I don't remember in what [NON-ENGLISH] [?
I had. ?] I only remember that my grandfather, my zayde, Nahum, gave me a little tiny silver.
That was my present.
